summary: Almost every old story starts the same way: there was a paradise, and we lost it. A garden, a tree that gave unending life, a forbidden thing, a serpent — and then exile, and death entering the world. The details rhyme from Israel to Sumer to Greece to the far north. Each remembers a time when people could have lived forever, and a single choice that took it away.
sharedMotifTags: a tree or plant of life

sources.provenance: The opening of the Hebrew Bible.
sources.quote: Then the LORD God said, “Behold, the man has become like one of Us, knowing good and evil; and now, he might reach out with his hand, and take fruit also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever”— therefore the LORD God sent him out of the Garden of Eden… So He drove the man out; and at the east of the Garden of Eden He stationed the cherubim and the flaming sword which turned every direction to guard the way to the tree of life.
sources.translation: New American Standard Bible (NASB), © The Lockman Foundation. Used by permission.
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: The clearest version: a garden, a tree of life, a serpent, a forbidden fruit, and humanity driven out specifically so it cannot eat from the tree of life and live forever.

sources.work: Epic of Gilgamesh — the plant of life
sources.provenance: The same epic that carries the Flood; Tablet XI.
sources.quote: Gilgamesh finds the plant that restores youth and would let a man cheat death — and while he bathes, a serpent slips up, eats the plant, sheds its skin, and slithers away. The one chance at unending life is taken by a snake.
sources.citation: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI (the plant 'the-old-man-becomes-young').
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: A serpent robbing humanity of immortality — the same two ingredients as Eden, in the oldest epic we have. The shed skin 'explains' why snakes seem to renew their lives while we don't.

sources.work: Adapa and the Food of Life; Dilmun
sources.provenance: Sumerian/Akkadian tradition; Dilmun is the pure paradise-land.
sources.quote: Adapa, the first sage, is offered the 'bread of life' and 'water of life' in heaven — but, warned to refuse, he turns it down and so forfeits immortality for all mankind. Dilmun, the garden-land, is described as pure, where the lion does not kill and there is no sickness or old age.
sources.translation: Summarized from the Adapa tablets and Enki & Ninhursag.
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: Humanity offered the food of immortality and losing it by a single choice — centuries before Genesis was written down.

sources.work: The Garden of the Hesperides; Pandora
sources.quote: At the world's western edge lies a divine garden whose tree grows golden apples of immortality — guarded by a serpent, Ladon, coiled around the trunk. And in Pandora's story, humanity once lived free of death and toil until a forbidden jar was opened and let suffering into the world.
sources.translation: Summarized from Hesiod's Theogony and Works and Days.
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: A guarded tree of immortal fruit with a serpent around it, and a 'golden age' lost through one forbidden act — the same shape again, in Greece.

sources.quote: The goddess Iðunn keeps the apples that the gods must eat to stay forever young. When she and her apples are stolen away, the gods immediately begin to grow old and grey — life unending depends on the fruit, and is lost the moment it is gone.
sources.citation: Prose Edda, Skáldskaparmál (Iðunn and her apples).
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: Even in the far north: a fruit of immortality, and aging/death the instant it's taken away.

sources.work: Samudra Manthan — the Churning of the Cosmic Ocean (Mahabharata; Bhagavata Purana)
sources.textRecorded: Mahabharata c. 4th c. BCE – 4th c. CE; Bhagavata Purana c. 9th–10th c. CE
sources.provenance: One of the central mythological episodes of Hinduism, shared across the Mahabharata, Ramayana and Puranas.
sources.quote: The gods and demons churn the cosmic ocean together — using the great serpent Vasuki coiled around Mount Mandara as a rope — to bring up Amrita, the nectar of immortality. When it surfaces, demons seize it and a contest erupts over who will possess undying life. The god Vishnu recovers it through divine intervention. The key elements recur exactly: immortality in the form of a divine substance, a serpent at the center of the act, a forbidden seizure, and mortals ultimately losing access to the undying life.
sources.translation: Summarized from the Mahabharata, Adi Parva, sections 17–18 (Samudra Manthan), and the Bhagavata Purana 8.5–12.
sources.citation: Mahabharata, Adi Parva 17–18 (Samudra Manthan); Bhagavata Purana 8.5–12.
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: The serpent is the churning rope; the nectar of immortality is the prize; and mortals are excluded from it through a divine contest. The structural match to the Eden and Gilgamesh patterns is exact — and the two traditions arose completely independently, thousands of miles apart.

sources.work: Xi Wangmu's Peach Garden — Shanhaijing; Mu Tianzi Zhuan
sources.survivor: the gods / King Mu of Zhou
sources.textRecorded: Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) c. 4th–2nd c. BCE; Mu Tianzi Zhuan c. 4th c. BCE
sources.provenance: Core Chinese mythological tradition of the immortality garden on Kunlun Mountain.
sources.quote: On Kunlun Mountain — the pillar at the world's center — the goddess Xi Wangmu (Queen Mother of the West) tends a divine garden where peaches of immortality ripen once every three thousand years. Eating one grants unending life. In the oldest texts the garden is inaccessible and its fruit forbidden to mortals; later stories tell of it being entered and the peaches stolen — the same sacred garden, the same life-giving fruit, the same barrier between mortal humans and immortality.
sources.translation: Summarized from the Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the Mu Tianzi Zhuan (Tale of King Mu).
sources.citation: Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas), passim; Mu Tianzi Zhuan (4th c. BCE); cf. Han Wu Gushi (story of the Han emperor visiting Xi Wangmu).
motifMatches: a tree or plant of life
sources.notes: On the opposite side of the world from Eden, China preserves the identical structure: a divine garden on the sacred mountain, a fruit that grants immortality, and a barrier keeping mortal humans from it. The tradition is as old as any Chinese written source — and arose with no possible contact with Mesopotamia or Israel.

sources.work: Airyana Vaejah and Yima — Avesta, Vendidad 1–2
sources.textRecorded: Avesta composed over many centuries; Vendidad compiled c. 1st–3rd c. CE from much older oral material attributed to Zarathustra (c. 1500–500 BCE)
sources.provenance: The Zoroastrian scriptures; Vendidad opens with the creation of the perfect first land.
sources.quote: Ahura Mazda declares Airyana Vaejah — 'the brilliant Aryan expanse' — the first and most beautiful land he ever made, a paradise of perfection where there is no sickness and no death. There Yima, the first mortal king, rules a golden age of abundance. Then Angra Mainyu (the evil spirit) poisons the paradise, bringing biting winter and barrenness, and the age ends. Yima then builds a great underground vara — a sealed sanctuary — to preserve the seed of every living thing from the catastrophe to come.
sources.translation: Summarized from Vendidad, Fargard 1–2; James Darmesteter translation, Sacred Books of the East vol. 4 (1880), public domain.
sources.citation: Avesta, Vendidad 1–2; James Darmesteter trans., Sacred Books of the East vol. 4 (Oxford, 1880).
sources.notes: Persia's first man lives in a deathless paradise until evil enters and drives him out — and his response (building a vara to preserve all life inside it) knits the Garden story directly into the Flood. 'Yima' is the Iranian cognate of Vedic 'Yama' — the same ancient Indo-Iranian figure, remembered on both sides of a continent.

science.intro: There's little to dig up for a garden. But the texts make checkable claims — that all people descend from one first pair, that paradise sat between named rivers, that farming and cities began right there. Here's where the data lands.

evidence.title: All of us go back to one woman, and one man
evidence.observation: Genetics traces every living human's maternal line to a single woman ('Mitochondrial Eve') and every paternal line to a single man ('Y-chromosomal Adam').
evidence.tie: The text says humanity began with one pair. The DNA of every person alive really does converge back to a single original mother and father line.

evidence.title: The fourth river that dried up
evidence.observation: Genesis names four rivers of Eden; two (Tigris, Euphrates) are obvious, but satellite imagery revealed a long-dry ancient riverbed in Arabia (the 'Kuwait River') matching a described fourth, near the head of the Persian Gulf.
evidence.tie: Archaeologist Juris Zarins placed Eden right there — where four rivers once met — exactly as the text lays it out.
evidence.source: J. Zarins' Eden hypothesis; LANDSAT-detected Wadi Al-Batin.

evidence.title: Farming and cities began exactly there
evidence.observation: The earliest agriculture, domestication, writing and cities all appear first in the same Tigris–Euphrates region the text calls humanity's starting point.
evidence.tie: If mankind began and was 'sent out to till the ground' there, that's precisely where civilization's footprints start.
evidence.source: Fertile Crescent origins of agriculture and cities.

evidence.title: Everyone remembers a lost paradise
evidence.observation: A primeval paradise and a fall from it recur from Sumer's Dilmun to Greece's Golden Age to traditions worldwide.
evidence.tie: A shared human memory of a perfect beginning we forfeited — carried by peoples who never met.
